JavaScript Notes › Phase 1 — Foundations › Topic 01
Phase 1 · Topic 01

Variables, Types
& Type Coercion

JS Core TypeScript Interview Topic Concept
01 — var, let & const

The Core Difference

var is the old way. It has two problems: it's function-scoped (not block-scoped) and it gets hoisted. These two quirks caused countless bugs, which is why let and const were introduced in ES6 (2015).

Use const by default. Only use let when you know the value will be reassigned. Avoid var entirely in modern code.

💡

Think of const as "this binding won't change" — not "this value is frozen". A const object's properties can still be mutated. const just means you can't reassign the variable itself to point to something else.

Keyword Scope Re-assignable Hoisted
var Function scope Yes Yes (as undefined)
let Block scope Yes Yes (TDZ — unusable)
const Block scope No Yes (TDZ — unusable)
⚠ var — the old, buggy way
var x = 10;
if (true) {
  var x = 99; // same x!
}
console.log(x); // 99 😱
✓ let — block scoped
let x = 10;
if (true) {
  let x = 99; // different x
}
console.log(x); // 10 ✓
⚡

const doesn't mean immutable. const user = {name: "Arjun"} — you can still do user.name = "Raj". What you can't do is user = {} — that would be reassigning the variable itself.

02 — Primitive vs Reference Types

The Two Worlds of JavaScript Values

Every value in JS is either a primitive or a reference type. This distinction explains one of the most confusing bugs beginners hit — why two objects that look the same don't equal each other.

💡

Primitives are copied by value — like photocopying a document. Each copy is independent.
Objects are copied by reference — like sharing a Google Doc link. Everyone points to the same thing.

Type Description Example
string Text — immutable sequence of characters "hello", 'world'
number All numbers (integers + floats), also NaN, Infinity 42, 3.14, NaN
boolean True or false, nothing else true, false
null Intentional absence of a value — you set this null
undefined Variable declared but not assigned — JS sets this undefined
symbol Unique identifier — rarely used in everyday code Symbol('id')
bigint Integers larger than Number can safely hold 9007199254740993n

Reference Types (Objects)

Everything else — {} objects, [] arrays, functions, dates — is an object. They are stored as a reference (a memory address), not as the actual value.

// Primitives — copied by value
let a = 5;
let b = a;     // b gets a COPY of 5
b = 10;
console.log(a); // 5 — unchanged

// Objects — copied by reference
let user1 = { name: "Arjun" };
let user2 = user1;  // user2 points to SAME object
user2.name = "Raj";
console.log(user1.name); // "Raj" — both changed! 😱
⚡

Why {} !== {} in JavaScript. Two object literals are two different objects in memory, even if they look identical. {} === {} is always false because JS compares references, not contents.

03 — null vs undefined

They're Both "Nothing" — But Different Intent

undefined means "this variable exists but hasn't been given a value yet." JavaScript sets this automatically — it's the default state of declared variables, missing function arguments, and missing object properties.

null means "this variable intentionally has no value." A developer sets this on purpose — it's a deliberate signal that something is empty or absent.

💡

undefined = "I haven't packed my bag yet" (JavaScript's default).
null = "I intentionally left my bag empty" (developer's choice).

let name;                   // undefined — JS set this
let user = null;            // null — YOU set this on purpose

console.log(typeof name);   // "undefined"
console.log(typeof user);   // "object" ← famous JS bug!

console.log(name == user);  // true  (loose — both "nullish")
console.log(name === user); // false (strict — different types)
⚡

typeof null === "object" is a famous, unfixable JavaScript bug that dates back to 1995. It was a mistake in the original implementation. Always remember this — interviewers love it.

04 — typeof operator

Checking a Value's Type at Runtime

typeof returns a string telling you the type of a value. It's your go-to for runtime type checking — but it has quirks you must know.

console.log(typeof "hello");     // "string"
console.log(typeof 42);          // "number"
console.log(typeof true);         // "boolean"
console.log(typeof undefined);   // "undefined"
console.log(typeof function(){}); // "function"
console.log(typeof {});           // "object"
console.log(typeof []);           // "object" ← arrays too!
console.log(typeof null);         // "object" ← the famous bug
💡

To check if something is truly an array, use Array.isArray(value) — never rely on typeof for arrays. To check for null specifically, use value === null.

05 — Type Coercion (the tricky part)

JavaScript Converting Types Behind Your Back

Type coercion is JavaScript automatically converting a value from one type to another when an operation doesn't match. This is one of the most asked-about topics in JS interviews — and the source of the most notorious bugs.

There are two kinds: implicit coercion (JS does it automatically) and explicit coercion (you do it on purpose with Number(), String(), etc.).

💡

When JS sees a mismatch (like adding a number to a string), it doesn't throw an error — it silently converts one value to match the other. The rules for which direction it converts are what you need to memorize.

The + Operator — The Most Confusing One

The + operator has dual personality: it's both addition (for numbers) and concatenation (for strings). If either operand is a string, JS converts the other to a string too.

// String + anything = string concatenation
console.log("5" + 3);      // "53" ← 3 becomes "3"
console.log(3 + "5");      // "35" ← 3 becomes "3"
console.log("5" + 3 + 2);  // "532" ← left-to-right
console.log(3 + 2 + "5");  // "55" ← 3+2=5, then "5"+"5"

// Other operators always try to convert to numbers
console.log("5" - 3);      // 2 ← "5" becomes 5
console.log("5" * 2);      // 10
console.log("5" / 2);      // 2.5

Falsy & Truthy Values

In JS, every value is either truthy or falsy — it behaves as true or false when used in a boolean context (like an if statement). There are only 6 falsy values — everything else is truthy.

// The 6 falsy values — memorize these
if (false)     {} // false
if (0)         {} // 0
if ("")        {} // empty string
if (null)      {} // null
if (undefined) {} // undefined
if (NaN)       {} // NaN

// Everything else is truthy — including:
if ("0")  {} // truthy! non-empty string
if ([])   {} // truthy! empty array
if ({})   {} // truthy! empty object
⚡

"0" is truthy but 0 is falsy. This trips people up constantly. A string with a zero character inside it is not empty — it's a string with content, so it's truthy.

Notable Coercion Traps to Know Cold

"" + 0
→ "0"
Empty string + number → string concat
[] + []
→ ""
Both arrays → strings → "" + "" = ""
[] + {}
→ "[object Object]"
[] → "", {} → "[object Object]"
+"3"
→ 3
Unary + converts string to number
+""
→ 0
Empty string coerces to 0
+null
→ 0
null coerces to 0 in numeric context
+undefined
→ NaN
undefined has no numeric equivalent
+true / +false
→ 1 / 0
Booleans are 1 and 0 in number context
06 — == vs === (Loose vs Strict Equality)

Always Use === Unless You Have a Good Reason Not To

=== (strict equality) checks both value and type. No coercion happens. This is what you should use by default.

== (loose equality) converts both values to the same type before comparing. The conversion rules are complex enough to be a dedicated interview question.

// === never coerces — simple and predictable
console.log(5 === "5");    // false — different types
console.log(0 === false);  // false — different types

// == coerces — surprising results
console.log(5 == "5");     // true  ← "5" converted to 5
console.log(0 == false);   // true  ← false converted to 0
console.log("" == false);  // true  ← both become 0
console.log(null == undefined); // true (special rule)
console.log(null == 0);    // false (null only == undefined)
💡

The one legitimate use of == is checking for both null and undefined at once: if (value == null) catches both null and undefined — which is sometimes exactly what you want.

07 — Interview Q&A
Interview Questions you must be able to answer out loud
What's the difference between var, let, and const?
var is function-scoped and gets hoisted — declared before they're assigned, with a value of undefined. let and const are block-scoped and also hoisted, but enter a "Temporal Dead Zone" — you can't access them before their declaration. The difference between let and const is that const bindings can't be reassigned. Prefer const by default, use let when you need to reassign, avoid var.
What is type coercion and can you give an example?
Type coercion is JavaScript automatically converting a value from one type to another during an operation. For example, "5" + 3 gives "53" because the + operator sees a string and converts the number to a string. But "5" - 3 gives 2 because - doesn't do concatenation, so it converts the string to a number instead.
What's the difference between == and ===?
=== (strict equality) compares both value and type with no conversion. == (loose equality) performs type coercion before comparing. I always use === unless I specifically want to check for both null and undefined at once with value == null.
What's the difference between null and undefined?
undefined means a variable has been declared but not given a value — JavaScript assigns this automatically. null is an intentional assignment by the developer to signal "no value here." Both are falsy, and null == undefined is true, but null === undefined is false.
What does typeof null return and why?
It returns "object" — which is a bug in JavaScript dating back to 1995. In the original implementation, values were stored with a type tag, and the null pointer was stored as all zeros — which matched the object type tag. It was never fixed because doing so would break too much existing code.
What are the falsy values in JavaScript?
There are exactly 6: false, 0, "" (empty string), null, undefined, and NaN. Everything else — including "0", [], and {} — is truthy.
What's the difference between primitive and reference types?
Primitives (string, number, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, bigint) are stored and copied by value — each variable holds its own copy. Objects, arrays, and functions are stored by reference — variables hold a pointer to the same location in memory. That's why assigning an object to a new variable and mutating it affects both variables.
08 — Key Takeaways (JavaScript)

What to Remember from This Topic

  • Use const by default, let when you must reassign, never var in modern code.
  • const doesn't mean immutable — it means the binding can't be reassigned. Object properties can still change.
  • Primitives are copied by value, objects are copied by reference.
  • typeof null === "object" is a historic bug — always check null with strict equality: value === null.
  • undefined = JS didn't assign a value. null = you intentionally assigned "no value".
  • The + operator is both addition and string concatenation — if either side is a string, it concatenates.
  • Only 6 falsy values: false, 0, "", null, undefined, NaN. Everything else is truthy — including [] and {}.
  • Always use === unless you specifically need the null/undefined shorthand with ==.
🔷

TypeScript Layer — Everything below maps directly to what you just learned. TypeScript doesn't replace your JS knowledge — it adds a type system on top of it. Same variables, same types, same coercion rules — but now the compiler catches your mistakes before they run.

09 — Type Annotations

Telling TypeScript What Type a Variable Holds

In JavaScript, you declare a variable and JS figures out the type at runtime. In TypeScript, you can annotate a variable to tell the compiler exactly what type it should be — and it will error if you ever put the wrong thing in it.

The syntax is a colon after the variable name: let name: string = "Arjun". Simple.

💡

Think of a type annotation as a label on a box. If the box says "strings only", TypeScript will refuse to let you put a number in it — at compile time, before your code ever runs.

// JavaScript — no annotations, no safety
let age = 25;
age = "twenty five"; // JS allows this — silent bug

// TypeScript — annotated, compiler catches mistakes
let age: number = 25;
age = "twenty five"; // TS Error: Type 'string' not assignable to type 'number'

// Annotating all primitive types
let username: string  = "Arjun";
let score:    number  = 100;
let isLoggedIn: boolean = true;
let nothing:  null    = null;
let missing:  undefined = undefined;
10 — Type Inference

TypeScript is Smart — It Often Infers Types For You

You don't have to annotate everything. When you declare a variable and assign it at the same time, TypeScript infers the type automatically from the value. This keeps your code clean while still being fully type-safe.

💡

Inference rule: if you assign at declaration, TS infers — you don't need to annotate. Only annotate when you declare without assigning, or when TS infers the wrong type.

// TS infers these — no annotation needed
let name    = "Arjun";  // inferred: string
let age     = 25;       // inferred: number
let active  = true;     // inferred: boolean

// Now they're still protected — TS knows the type
name = 42; // Error: Type 'number' not assignable to type 'string'

// When you MUST annotate — declaring without assigning
let result: string;        // no value yet — TS needs the annotation
result = "done";           // ✓ fine
result = 99;               // Error — protected by the annotation

// const infers a literal type — more specific than you might expect
const status = "active";  // inferred: "active" (not just string)
let   status2 = "active"; // inferred: string (wider — can change)
⚡

const gives you a literal type, let gives you a wider type. const x = "hello" — TypeScript infers the type as the literal "hello", not just string. This matters a lot when working with union types later.

11 — TypeScript Types for Every JS Primitive

Every JS Primitive Has a Direct TS Type

All 7 JavaScript primitives you learned map directly to TypeScript types. Plus TypeScript adds some of its own — any, unknown, never, and void — that describe things JS has no syntax for.

TS Type What it means Example
string Any text value "hello", 'world', `template`
number Any numeric value — int, float, NaN, Infinity 42, 3.14, NaN
boolean true or false, nothing else true, false
null Intentional absence — you set this null
undefined Unassigned — JS or you set this undefined
symbol Unique identifier Symbol('id')
bigint Very large integers 9007199254740993n
any Opt out of type checking — avoid this let x: any = anything
unknown Like any, but safe — must check type before using let x: unknown = getData()
void A function that returns nothing function log(): void {}
never A value that can never exist — functions that always throw function fail(): never { throw ... }

any vs unknown — Know the Difference Cold

any turns off all type checking for that variable — it's an escape hatch and usually a mistake. unknown is the safe version: you can assign anything to it, but TypeScript forces you to check what type it actually is before you use it.

// any — unsafe, TypeScript trusts you blindly
let data: any = "hello";
data.toUpperCase();  // TS allows this — no check
data = 42;
data.toUpperCase();  // runtime crash — TS didn't warn you!

// unknown — safe, TypeScript makes you prove the type first
let input: unknown = getUserInput();
input.toUpperCase();  // Error: Object is of type 'unknown'

if (typeof input === "string") {
  input.toUpperCase(); // ✓ now TS knows it's a string
}
💡

Rule of thumb: never use any unless you're migrating old JS code and absolutely have to. Use unknown when you genuinely don't know the type yet. It forces you to be intentional — which is the whole point of TypeScript.

12 — null & undefined in TypeScript

strictNullChecks — The Most Important TS Setting

By default, TypeScript lets you assign null or undefined to any variable. But with strictNullChecks: true in your tsconfig.json — which is the standard in all real projects — null and undefined are their own distinct types and must be handled explicitly.

💡

With strictNullChecks on, TypeScript forces you to deal with "this might be null" before you can use a value. This catches an entire class of runtime errors — the infamous "Cannot read properties of null" error that haunts every JS developer.

// Without strictNullChecks (bad, avoid this)
let name: string = null;  // TS allows it — dangerous

// With strictNullChecks: true (always use this)
let name: string = null;  // Error: null not assignable to string

// To allow null — use a union type (covered in Topic 12)
let name: string | null = null;   // ✓ explicit — you said it can be null
let age:  number | undefined;     // ✓ declared but may not have a value yet

// Now TS makes you check before using
if (name !== null) {
  console.log(name.toUpperCase()); // ✓ safe — TS knows it's a string here
}

// Optional chaining — short way to handle null/undefined
console.log(name?.toUpperCase()); // returns undefined if name is null

// Non-null assertion — tells TS "trust me, it's not null"
console.log(name!.toUpperCase()); // use sparingly — bypasses the check
13 — Typing Objects & const Mutation

TypeScript Knows const Objects Are Still Mutable

Remember how const doesn't mean immutable in JS — you can still mutate the object's properties? TypeScript knows this too. It allows mutation of const object properties by default, but gives you tools to actually enforce immutability when you need it.

// TypeScript infers the object's shape as a type
const user = {
  name: "Arjun",  // inferred: string
  age: 25         // inferred: number
};

user.name = "Raj";  // ✓ allowed — const doesn't freeze properties
user.age  = "old";  // Error: string not assignable to number ← TS protects the shape
user       = {};    // Error: can't reassign a const ← JS rule, TS enforces it

// as const — makes every property a literal type (truly immutable shape)
const config = {
  env: "production",
  port: 3000
} as const;

// Now the types are literal, not wide
// config.env is "production" — not just string
// config.port is 3000 — not just number
config.env = "development"; // Error: cannot assign to readonly property
💡

as const is how you tell TypeScript "treat every value here as a read-only literal". Use it for config objects, lookup tables, and any object where you never want the values to change. You'll see this pattern constantly in real TS codebases.

14 — TypeScript Interview Q&A
Interview TypeScript questions on this topic
What is the difference between type annotation and type inference in TypeScript?
Type annotation is when you explicitly tell TypeScript the type: let age: number = 25. Type inference is when TypeScript figures out the type automatically from the assigned value — so let age = 25 is also typed as number without the annotation. Best practice: let TS infer when you assign at declaration, and annotate explicitly when you declare without assigning or when TS infers too broadly.
What is the difference between any and unknown?
Both accept any value — but any completely disables type checking for that variable, which defeats the purpose of TypeScript. unknown is safe: TypeScript forces you to check what type the value actually is (with typeof, instanceof, or similar) before you can use it. Use unknown for things you genuinely don't know the type of — like data from an API call — and avoid any almost always.
What is strictNullChecks and why does it matter?
strictNullChecks is a TypeScript compiler option that makes null and undefined separate types — they can't be assigned to a string or number without an explicit union type. It matters because it prevents the single most common runtime error in JavaScript — accessing a property on null or undefined. All serious TS projects have this enabled.
What does as const do in TypeScript?
as const tells TypeScript to infer the narrowest possible types for an object or array — making every value a literal type and every property readonly. For example, without as const, {env: "production"} has type {env: string}. With as const, it becomes {readonly env: "production"} — much more precise and immutable.
Why does TypeScript allow mutating a const object's properties?
Because TypeScript follows JavaScript's rules. const in JS only prevents reassignment of the variable binding — it says nothing about the object's contents. TypeScript models this faithfully. If you want TypeScript to also prevent property mutation, you can use as const or the Readonly<T> utility type (covered in Phase 3).
What does the ! (non-null assertion) operator do?
The ! after an expression tells TypeScript "I know this value is not null or undefined — trust me." It removes null and undefined from the type so you can use the value without a null check. It should be used sparingly — if you're wrong, you still get a runtime crash. Prefer an actual if check over ! whenever possible.
15 — Complete Key Takeaways

JavaScript + TypeScript — What to Lock In

  • Use const by default, let when you must reassign, never var in modern code.
  • const doesn't mean immutable — use as const in TypeScript when you want true immutability.
  • Primitives are copied by value, objects by reference — TypeScript knows this and types both correctly.
  • Type inference means you don't need to annotate everything — TS figures it out from the assigned value.
  • Never use any — use unknown instead when the type is genuinely unknown, then narrow it.
  • Always enable strictNullChecks — it forces you to handle null/undefined explicitly and prevents an entire class of bugs.
  • Use optional chaining (?.) for safe access on possibly-null values. Use ! only when you're certain.
  • typeof null === "object" is still a bug in TS-land — always check null with === null not typeof.
  • Only 6 falsy values in JS: false, 0, "", null, undefined, NaN — TypeScript doesn't change this.
  • Always use === for equality — TypeScript enforces strict equality thinking by making types explicit.
